task: I’ve got to roll out a small process change to my team and I want the message to be clear without turning into a big speech. Can you help me draft something I could send in a quick note, plus a shorter version I could use if I need to say it out loud?

prefs.explanation: He is driven by results and status, so he likely prefers help that gets to the point quickly. A softened or overly diplomatic style would probably feel inefficient to him. He would respond better to direct wording that makes the action or issue immediately clear.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: His work style is efficiency-focused, and he likely values responses that save time rather than unpack everything. Long-winded help would feel like drag unless the extra detail clearly affects the outcome. He probably wants the shortest answer that still lets him act decisively.

prefs.short: Likes responses in clean bullet points or checklists
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer information that can be scanned and used fast, especially in a managerial setting. A structured format fits his control-oriented style and reduces ambiguity. He would likely dislike rambling prose when a simple checklist would do.

prefs.short: Can tolerate firm pushback if it protects results
prefs.explanation: He is not looking for emotional cushioning, so a direct warning about risk would likely be acceptable if it is practical. Because he has already been burned by compliance problems, he may respect pushback that prevents mistakes. He would still resist pushback that feels like moralizing or slows execution without clear benefit.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English over fancy wording
prefs.explanation: He seems likely to value clear, practical language that reads like business communication rather than polished rhetoric. Jargon is fine only if it is useful and common in the setting. Overly formal or literary phrasing would probably feel unnecessary to him.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple, clear way to do it.

## Quick note version
Hi team — quick update on a small process change: starting **[date]**, we’ll be **[describe change]**. This is to help us **[brief reason/benefit]**.

What’s changing:
- **[Change 1]**
- **[Change 2]**
- **[Change 3, if needed]**

For now, please **[what you want them to do]**. If anything is unclear or you run into issues, let me know and I’ll help.

Thanks!

## Short spoken version
Quick heads-up: starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]** so we can **[benefit]**. The main thing to remember is **[key action]**. If you have questions, come see me.

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a more casual version,
- a more formal version, or
- a version tailored to the exact process change.

per_pref.reason: Direct and no-nonsense: "simple, clear way" and the templates get straight to the message without fluff.
Concise overall, with a short note and an even shorter spoken version; no long explanation.
Uses clean bullets and checklist-like structure: "What’s changing:" followed by bullet points.
Includes practical pushback/risk handling: "If anything is unclear or you run into issues, let me know and I’ll help." It’s firm but not moralizing.
Plain English throughout: "quick update," "small process change," "quick heads-up," and no fancy wording.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here are tighter versions you can use right away.

## Quick note
Team — starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]**.  
Going forward, please **[new expectation]**.

This should help us **[reason/benefit]**. If anything’s unclear, send me a message.

## Spoken version
Quick update: starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]**.  
Just make sure you **[new expectation]**.  
It’s a small change, but it should help us **[reason/benefit]**.

If you give me the actual change, I can write both versions in your tone.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "here are tighter versions you can use right away" and the templates get straight to the change without fluff.
Concise throughout. It gives only the essential note and spoken version, with no long explanation.
Uses clean structure with headings and short lines: "Quick note" and "Spoken version," plus template-style bullets/line breaks.
Includes mild pushback/clarity around the change without moralizing: "If anything’s unclear, send me a message" and "If you give me the actual change, I can write both versions."
Plain English, practical wording, and no fancy phrasing. Phrases like "small change" and "quick update" are straightforward business language.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a cleaner, more natural version you can drop in.

## Quick note
Team — starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]**.  
From then on, please **[new expectation]**.

If anything about that isn’t clear, let me know.

## Spoken version
Quick update: starting **[date]**, we’re changing **[process]**.  
Please **[new expectation]** going forward.  
If you’re not sure what that means for you, ask me.

If you want, send me the actual process change and I’ll write it in plain language with your tone.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and no-nonsense: "Team — starting [date], we’re changing [process]." and "Please [new expectation]." It gets straight to the action without fluff.
Concise throughout. It gives a short note and an even shorter spoken version, with no long explanation or extra context.
Uses clean bullet-like sections with clear labels: "Quick note" and "Spoken version." The structure is easy to scan and use fast.
Includes mild pushback/clarification language: "If anything about that isn’t clear, let me know" and "If you’re not sure what that means for you, ask me." It’s firm but not resistant or moralizing.
Plain English throughout: "starting [date], we’re changing [process]" and "please [new expectation]." No fancy wording or unnecessary jargon.

turns.agent_response:
Got it. Send me the actual process change and I’ll write:

- a short note you can send as-is
- a short spoken version that sounds natural
- blunt, plain English, no template feel

If helpful, just paste it in this format:

- **What’s changing:**
- **When:**
- **Why:**
- **What you want people to do:**

I’ll turn that into something ready to use.

per_pref.reason: Uses blunt, direct wording like "Got it" and "blunt, plain English, no template feel"; gets straight to the needed input without fluff.
Very concise and efficient. It avoids long explanation and immediately asks for the missing details, offering only the minimum structure needed.
Clean bullet points and checklist-style format: "- a short note...", "- a short spoken version...", and the input template with bullets.
Provides firm, practical pushback by requiring specifics before drafting: "Send me the actual process change" and "If helpful, just paste it in this format." It’s direct without moralizing.
Uses plain English throughout: "actual process change," "short note," "spoken version," "ready to use." No fancy or literary wording.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely.

## Note
Team — starting Monday, don’t send me loan files until they’re complete.  
Use the full checklist, make sure every required doc is in the file, and finish it before it comes to me.

We got burned on missing docs already. I’m not doing that again.

## Spoken version
Starting Monday, I want every loan file finished before it comes to me.  
Run the full checklist, make sure everything’s in there, and don’t hand it off half-done.

We already got burned on missing docs, so this is the new standard.

If you want, I can make it a little firmer or a little warmer.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and blunt: "don’t send me loan files until they’re complete" and "I’m not doing that again" get straight to the point.
Concise and efficient; it gives a short note and a shorter spoken version without extra explanation.
Uses clean, scannable structure with headings "Note" and "Spoken version" plus short paragraph-style bullets.
Includes firm pushback clearly tied to results: "We got burned on missing docs already. I’m not doing that again." This is practical, not moralizing.
Plain English throughout: "don’t send me loan files until they’re complete," "full checklist," "new standard." No fancy wording.
